banner: Week 39 · Capstone Build — Write It Up for the Portfolio
essential_question: Can you write up your capstone so someone who wasn't there believes every number?
objective: Write up the three-part capstone for the Portfolio — presenting the trig measurement, the solid's volume/surface area, and the transformation — with a short proof that the transformation preserved (or predictably changed) length and angle.

content.prompt: Reread your Build Day measurements from Session 2. Circle any number you're not fully sure of before you write it up.

content.heading: Make it checkable
content.note: Write a short paragraph presenting your object, your angle and baseline, your equation, and your answer with units — clearly enough that someone else could redo your math and get the same number.

content.heading: Show the formulas
content.note: Write a short paragraph presenting your solid, its dimensions, and your volume/surface-area calculations — and how they compared to what you actually built or measured.

agenda.title: Prove It — Part 3: The Transformation Proof

content.heading: Defend the claim
bullets: State exactly what transformation (or sequence) you performed.
Write a short proof — two-column or paragraph — that it preserved side length and angle (or, if a dilation, that it preserved angle and scaled every length by a constant ratio).
Use the definition of your transformation (or CPCTC, if you compared triangles) as your reason.

success_criteria.text: I can write up a real-world trig measurement clearly enough that someone else could check it
I can write up a solid's volume and surface area with the formulas and numbers that support them
I can write a short proof that my transformation preserved (or predictably changed) length and angle

questions.prompt: A transformation that preserves both side length and angle measure is called a…
questions.explain: Translations, reflections, and rotations are rigid motions — they preserve every distance and angle.

questions.prompt: Complete the proof. Given: triangle ABC is reflected over the y-axis to form triangle A'B'C'. Prove: triangle ABC ≅ triangle A'B'C'. Statements: (1) Triangle ABC is reflected over the y-axis to form A'B'C'. (2) A reflection is a rigid motion — it preserves every distance and angle measure. (3) AB = A'B', BC = B'C', AC = A'C', and all corresponding angles are congruent. (4) Triangle ABC ≅ Triangle A'B'C'. Fill in the REASON for each of the four statements above.
questions.answer: (1) Given. (2) Definition/property of a reflection (a reflection is a rigid motion). (3) Definition of a rigid motion — distance and angle measure are preserved. (4) Definition of congruent triangles (SSS, since all corresponding sides and angles match).
questions.explain: This is the general argument behind why any rigid transformation produces a congruent image — the transformation's own definition IS the reason at every step.

questions.prompt: Write one sentence stating your Part 1 result the way it should appear in your portfolio (object, method, answer with units).
questions.answer: Open-ended, e.g.: 'Using an angle of elevation of 31° measured from 40 ft away, the height of the oak tree was found to be 40·tan(31°) ≈ 24 ft.'

questions.prompt: Close: submit your photo/CAD file and write-up to the Portfolio ([[Records/Portfolio]]), then rate your overall mastery for the year and name the one geometry idea you're proudest of proving.
